title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
SEO Insertion on What is a participating and non-participating policy? - Article Page
Keyword density and no. of target keywords has been mentioned as comments in the beginning
Suggested additional anchor texts are marked as comments in Yellow. (If required)
Keywords: participating policy, non-participating policy, unit linked insurance plan, importance of life insurance
Body Content:
As is obvious, there’s no ‘one-size-fits-all’ life insurance product. The various kinds of plans available in the market are designed to fulfil different types of insurance needs and life goals.
While you may be aware of the importance of a life insurance plan in safeguarding the financial future of your loved ones, you might still need some clarity on the various types of insurance products available in the market and what they have to offer.
When browsing through insurance plans, you will surely come across participative and non-participative categorisations. Knowing about the nitty-gritty of each is essential to understanding which one is right for you. To help you do that, we’ve explained each of these policies below:
Broad Definitions
With a participating policy, you get to own a share of the profits the insurance company makes in a financial year. In simple words, such policies are with-profit plans. The profit payout generally accrues to you yearly in the form of bonuses or dividends. Remember that this profit value is over and above the maturity benefit that your policy promises.
However, in the case of a non-participating policy, you will not be entitled to a share in the company’s profits. Thus, no yearly dividend or bonus will accrue to you if you sign up for a without-profit policy.  While you might still receive guaranteed maturity benefits under such plans, you will not be entitled to any yearly profit-based bonuses.
Payment Frequency
As already mentioned, for participating policies, you will receive the profit shares as annualised bonuses. Thus, you will receive a profit payout every year from such plans which can then be used to meet your expenses or invested back into the plan to earn further interests. However, non-participating policies do not offer any such bonus payments.
Guaranteed Payments
Both these insurance policies offer certain guaranteed benefits. While the death benefit payout and survival/maturity payout from both plans can be classed as guaranteed benefits, participatory plans also come with a set of non-guaranteed benefits.
Essentially, the yearly bonus that you stand to earn from such plans will depend on the market and the overall performance of the company. Thus, these dividends and bonuses can be grouped under the non-guaranteed payout section, and their proportion may vary as per the market performance. Since non-participating plans don’t have a profit angle, there are only guaranteed payments under such policies.
Degree of Flexibility
A participating plan is characterised by a higher degree of flexibility than its non-participating counterpart. For instance, participating policies like unit-linked insurance plans, allow you to reap dual benefits of a life cover and returns but also allow you to redirect funds as per your changing preferences and risk appetite.
Alternatively, since the benefit structure and payout amount for a non-participating plan are fixed at the very onset, you don’t have much scope for changes. This makes them inflexible insurance products when compared to participating policies.
Risk and Returns
It is important to remember that while participating policies, like unit-linked insurance plans, may offer you a higher rate of return, they also come with a higher risk factor. For instance, due to sudden market transitions and fluctuations, if your insurance company does not perform well in a given fiscal year, the value of your bonus allocations will inevitably fall. This makes such policies riskier and their returns more insecure.
However, if you own a non-participating policy, you can rest assured that your maturity benefits are well-protected from the ebbs and flows of the market. The absence of market-linked payouts makes these plans less risky and their returns more secure.
Benefits Attached
When assessing the importance of these life insurance policies, you must also consider the benefit bundle that each such plan has to offer. For instance, both policies will offer your nominees the death benefit payout if something untoward were to happen to you during the policy term. Similarly, if you survive the term, you will be entitled to the maturity benefits listed under the policy.
However, under a non-participating plan, you will only receive these guaranteed benefits. For a participating plan, your benefits bundle will comprise of these guaranteed payouts as well as annualised dividends as a share of the company’s profits. Thus, with such plans, you get a little more that you can use to meet your short-term life goals or reinvest them for higher interests to meet your long-term goals.
The Bottom Line
You need to weigh these differences against each other to decide which plan is appropriate for you. While non-participating plans have lower risks, they may not be well-suited for those who wish to build a sizable corpus for the future.
Participating policies, like ULIPs, make for excellent options as they offer both insurance and investment benefits with the option to tweak your portfolio as per your changing preferences. Moreover, with the higher market-linked returns, you also get to enjoy tax exemptions, while the overall cash value of your policy is protected by the insurance company’s assets.
